planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil in full sun; cosmos blooms most prolifically in lean, dry soil — rich, moist conditions produce excessive foliage at the expense of flowers.

directions: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil in full sun; cosmos blooms most prolifically in lean, dry soil — rich, moist conditions produce excessive foliage at the expense of flowers. Cosmos bipinnatus is one of the most beloved, easy-to-grow garden annuals, producing feathery, thread-fine leaves on branching stems and a long succession of daisy-like flowers 2–4 inches across in white, pink, rose, burgundy, and bicolors from early summer through frost; individual plants bloom for 3–4 months when kept deadheaded. It is a superb butterfly plant, beloved by monarchs, swallowtails, and skippers, and a major nectar source for bumblebees and honeybees; it self-seeds freely in disturbed soil. Minimal care required — simply thin to 9–18 inches and let it bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; zinnia germinates in 5–7 days and reaches bloom in 60–70 days.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; zinnia germinates in 5–7 days and reaches bloom in 60–70 days. Zinnia elegans is the most important annual butterfly plant in the American garden — in high summer, flowering zinnias attract more butterfly species simultaneously than almost any other plant, hosting swallowtails, monarchs, painted ladies, fritillaries, and dozens of others; they are also the longest-blooming, most heat-resistant summer annual for zones 9–11. Plants bloom continuously from early summer through frost with deadheading, in a full palette of white, yellow, orange, red, pink, and bicolor; they are outstanding as cut flowers. Powdery mildew can be a problem in humid conditions — ensure good air circulation and choose mildew-resistant varieties for zone 8–9.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after all danger of frost has passed in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; marigolds transplant easily and bloom within 6–8 weeks of transplanting.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after all danger of frost has passed in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; marigolds transplant easily and bloom within 6–8 weeks of transplanting. Tagetes patula is the compact, bushy French marigold with small, double or single flowers in yellow, orange, red, and bicolors that bloom continuously from planting through frost; it has a long history as a companion plant for vegetable gardens, and research has confirmed that high-density plantings suppress root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne species) in subsequent seasons. The pungent fragrance of the foliage deters some insect pests; the flowers provide nectar for small native bees, hoverflies, and butterflies; and the plant is heat-tolerant, drought-resistant, and virtually care-free. Deadheading is not required but extends the display.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; sow no earlier than 6 weeks before last frost, as large-seeded transplants tend to become rootbound quickly.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; sow no earlier than 6 weeks before last frost, as large-seeded transplants tend to become rootbound quickly. Tagetes erecta is the tall, large-flowered marigold with enormous double pompom flower heads 3–4 inches across in brilliant yellow, orange, and cream — sacred to the goddess Xochiquetzal in Aztec culture and still used extensively in Mexican Day of the Dead (Día de Muertos) festivals, where millions of flowers are harvested annually. The petals are edible and have a mild, spicy, slightly citrusy flavor; dried petals are used to color butter, cheese, and poultry feed (providing the beta-carotene that gives egg yolks their golden color in commercial farming). It is the most important commercial cut marigold species; choose large transplant sizes in spring as they establish quickly.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep and 6–12 inches apart after last frost in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; sunflowers are extremely fast-growing and drought-tolerant once established but need consistent moisture during germination.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep and 6–12 inches apart after last frost in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; sunflowers are extremely fast-growing and drought-tolerant once established but need consistent moisture during germination. Helianthus annuus is a North American native wildflower domesticated by indigenous peoples in what is now the central United States before 3000 BCE as a seed oil, food, and dye crop long before its introduction to Europe; the wild plant is native to dry, open, disturbed sites from British Columbia to Mexico. In cultivation it produces large, ray-and-disk composite flowers from midsummer through fall that are premier nectar and pollen sources for native bees (particularly long-tongued bumblebees and specialist Melissodes bees) and produce abundant oil-rich seeds harvested by goldfinches, chickadees, and dozens of other birds. Ornamental, edible-seed, and bird-feeding selections range from 2-foot dwarfs to 14-foot giants; all are easy and rewarding.
